Table 8. Ablation studies and comparative analysis. (a) Impact of prompt ensembling on zero-shot performance across distinct templates. (b) Effect of varying the number of unfrozen Transformer blocks. (c) Performance comparison with UniMed-CLIP under equivalent data scaling (5.3M Images).

Figure 1: MedP-CLIP can be flexibly deployed for various downstream tasks, significantly enhancing the region-aware analysis capability of medical imaging. This model supports direct application and plug-and-play integration, and accepts explicit prompts from users as well as outputs from perceptual models.

Figure 12: t-SNE visualization of semantic embeddings from our synthesized clinical descriptions (red circles) and humanannotated RadGenome-Chest CT descriptions (green triangles) across 13 anatomical categories.

Figure 10: Exploration of model prompt sensitivity and boundary conditions. By comparing single point, multiple points, and bounding box interactions, this figure reveals the limitations of the model when dealing with out-of-distribution noise (left column) and ambiguous local features (middle column), using the right column as a baseline success reference.

Table 4. Region-level classification performance on the Br35H dataset across varying prompt types

Figure 4: Performance comparison of region-level classification on multi-organ datasets. “Δ” denotes the performance difference between Top-5 and Top-1. “Rect.” indicates rectangular region prompts generated from original masks via Alpha-CLIP.

Figure 2: MedP-CLIP: data, architecture and training. From IMed-361M we convert pixel-level organ/lesion masks into region–text pairs by generating prompts (point/box/mask) and GPT-4o based clinical descriptions (a). Dataset statistics across modalities, anatomies and mask ratios (b). Model pipeline: the image is encoded by a ViT, while the prompt is embedded by a lightweight Prompt Encoder (c). A feature-level Attention Block fuses dense and sparse prompt embeddings with image tokens via cross/self attention (d).
Figure 11: The relationship between MedP-CLIP pre-training data scaling and zero-shot classification.
